column1: Seconds in a day — the interview value
column2: 10^5 (exact: 86,400). Rounding under-counts QPS ~15%; the peak factor swallows it.
3 x 10^7 (~31.5M). For retention math: rate x size x 3x10^7.
2.5 x 10^6 (~2.6M). For monthly bills and rollups.

column1: X events per day = how many per second?
column2: Divide by 10^5. 1M/day = 10/s. 100M/day = 1k/s. 1B/day = 10k/s.
~10^3 (exact 1,024). The only binary/decimal bridge you need: 2^20 = 10^6, 2^30 = 10^9, 2^32 = 4 x 10^9.
Add exponents. 10^6 things x 10^3 bytes each = 10^9 = 1 GB. A million 1 KB things is a gigabyte.
125 MB/s (divide bits by 8). 10 Gbps = 1.25 GB/s. And 1 Gbps = ~10 TB/day.

column1: Default object sizes when you don't know
column2: 1 KB for a record, 1 MB for a media object — say it and move; refine later.

column1: Hours that carry ~80% of a region's traffic
column2: ~8 hours. Why a single-region peak factor is x5, not x3.
x3 global (diurnal bunching), x5 single-region (day compresses into ~8 h), x10+ event (flash sale, final over).

column1: Size: typical DB row / JSON payload / log line
column2: ~1 KB — a couple dozen fields plus index overhead. The default record.
~200 KB — a megapixel or two at 3 B/pixel, divided by ~20x JPEG.

column1: Size: original phone photo / with all variants
column2: ~2 MB original; ~2x with all stored variants = ~4 MB.
~50 MB single 1080p rendition; ~100 MB with all renditions stored. (1080p streams at ~5 Mbps = ~40 MB/min delivered.)

column1: Size: a web page, total transfer
column2: ~2 MB.
1 ns — the desk in front of you.
100 ns — off-chip; a DRAM row must charge. A shelf across the room.
50 us — sequential streaming at ~20 GB/s.
100 us — a NAND page read plus controller queueing. 1,000x a RAM touch.
1 ms.
0.5 ms — the fiber is ~5 us; switches and two kernels are the other 99%.
0.5 ms = the network round trip. A cache hit is a network operation; the hash lookup is noise.
5 ms — B-tree descent in a warm buffer pool, plus parse and protocol.
10 ms — an arm sweeps, a platter rotates underneath. Same as 1 MB over 1 Gbps.
50 ms / 250 ms. Cross-continent floor is physics: ~25,000 km round trip at two-thirds of c. No vendor shortens the Earth.
'Instant' = 100 ms (everything must fit inside it); 'I'll wait' limit = 1 s.
Each storage tier is ~3 orders of magnitude slower than the one above (RAM ns, SSD us-ms, network/disk ms). Designs argue about which tier the hot path touches.
Per commodity server (32 vCPU): ~100 QPS heavy work (~100 ms CPU), ~1,000 QPS typical logic (~10 ms), ~10,000 QPS trivial (~1 ms).

column1: Usable compute budget of a 32-core box
column2: ~10,000 core-ms per second: 32,000 core-ms x 0.6 utilization x ~0.5 in-process losses. Divide by ms-per-request to get QPS.
~5,000 QPS — warm B-tree reads cost a few core-ms each.
~1,000 TPS — every commit waits for fsync + replica ack, serialized. Writes are the wall.
A few TB practical — beyond that, shard.
~10,000 TPS — sequential appends now, compaction deferred to background.
~100,000 ops/s — ~10 us per op on one event-loop core. RAM-bound, not CPU-bound.
Plan ~100k (1M+ possible, tuned). A stock, not a rate — sockets cost RAM and file descriptors, not CPU.
NVMe ~3 GB/s and ~500k IOPS; HDD ~150 MB/s and ~150 IOPS.
25% default — the median user shows up one day in four. Sticky messengers: 50%.
~10% of DAU — each active user is present ~1.5 of ~16 waking hours.

column1: Read:write ratio — feeds, content, URL shortener
column2: 100:1 — one author, a hundred lurkers.
10:1 — most screens display, few submit.
~1:1 (then x fan-out) — each event written once, read about once.
20% of objects take 80% of reads. Popularity is a power law — the hot head is small enough to buy RAM for.
Cache = 20% of a day's read volume — the 80/20 working set absorbs most traffic.
Replication x3; indexes + metadata + headroom x1.5; together: logical bytes x5 = provisioned bytes (databases). Erasure-coded blob stores: x1.5.
Run at 60% — divide required capacity by 0.6. Running hot is running fragile.
Text /3 (logs /10); media already compressed, x1.
x2 per year — and say the assumption out loud.
99.9% = 8.8 h/year (44 min/month); 99.99% = 53 min/year (4.4 min/month).
Series: multiply (two 99.9% = 99.8%; four parties at three nines = 99.6%). Parallel: 1-(1-a)^2 (two 99% replicas = 99.99%).
~$30 per vCPU-month; the 32-core box = $1k/month. Price designs in boxes first.
Object (S3-class) ~$20/TB-month — storage is cheap. Block SSD ~$100/TB-month.
Egress ~$100/TB — egress runs the bill. CDN ~$30/TB — why CDNs exist, in one number.
~$15k/month fully loaded = fifteen 32-core boxes. The build-vs-buy unit; people dominate everything.
Average QPS = actions/day / 10^5. Peak = x3 (regional x5, event x10).
Storage = rate x size x retention x 5 (replication + overhead). Bandwidth = QPS x bytes per request, ingress and egress separately.
Servers = peak QPS / per-server QPS / 0.6. Shards = max(bytes / 2 TB, write TPS / node ceiling). Monthly cost = boxes x $1k + TB stored x $20 + TB egress x $100.
